summary: Uncle Bob demonstrates the Transformation Priority Premise (TPP) by implementing a flash card kata in Java, showing how to evolve code through small, prioritized transformations. Starting from an empty test, he incrementally builds a complete flash card game that tracks right and wrong answers. The post walks through specific transformations like ({}->nil), (nil->constant), (unconditional->if), and (if->while), showing how each moves the code forward. Along the way, he refactors to improve design, extracting a User interface and MockUser class. The exercise validates that following TPP's transformation priority leads to clean, correct algorithms without fighting the tests.
key_points: The Transformation Priority Premise provides a systematic approach to evolving code from simple to complex through prioritized transformations
Starting with the simplest possible test (empty list, zero counts) establishes the foundation for incremental development
Transformations like (nil->constant), (unconditional->if), and (if->while) each represent a specific, small step in code evolution
Refactoring is essential between transformations to keep code clean—the author repeatedly notes when code is 'ugly' or 'hideous' and addresses it
Following TPP naturally leads to correct algorithms: 'the algorithm came out nicely' without forcing or fighting the tests
money_quotes: We begin with an empty test which passes
In this case -1 is the integer equivalent of nil, e.g. a degenerate value
This solution is a bit ugly since it couples the test and the solution. So let's refactor to create a class.
Of course this is hideous, so we need to refactor alot.
This fails, but we can make it pass with a (if->while) transform.
There's more to do, of course, but the plumbing is all set up, and the algorithm looks right.
There were several cases where we could have used a lower transform such as (variable->assignment) but there was no need, and the algorithm came out nicely.
key_insight: The Transformation Priority Premise guides TDD practitioners through a systematic sequence of small code transformations that naturally evolve toward correct algorithms without fighting the tests.
